source: Customs Act 1962 — General Framework
title: Indian Customs — Governing Legislation and CBIC Authority
text: The Customs Act 1962 is the primary legislation governing the import and export of goods in India. It is administered by the Central Board of Indirect Taxes and Customs (CBIC) under the Ministry of Finance. All imported goods must pass through a customs port, airport, or land customs station notified under Section 7. The Act empowers customs officers to examine goods, demand documents, assess duty, detain or seize goods that contravene any provision, and arrest persons suspected of smuggling. Section 11 allows the Central Government to prohibit import or export of any goods in the public interest, including goods from specific countries. Importers must file a Bill of Entry (declaration of imported goods) within 30 days of the arrival of the vessel; failure to do so results in demurrage and possible abandonment proceedings.

source: Customs Tariff Act 1975 — ITC-HS Classification
title: ITC-HS Codes — 8-Digit Classification Requirement
text: India uses the Indian Trade Classification based on the Harmonized System (ITC-HS), an 8-digit commodity classification system maintained by DGFT. The first 6 digits align with the international HS system; digits 7 and 8 are India-specific. All Bills of Entry filed on ICEGATE must declare the 8-digit ITC-HS code for each line item. Incorrect classification is the most common cause of customs disputes, excess duty demands, and penalty proceedings. The Customs Tariff Act 1975 prescribes the Basic Customs Duty (BCD) rate for each ITC-HS heading. Classification disputes are first adjudicated by the customs officer; appeals lie to the Commissioner (Appeals) and then to the Customs, Excise and Service Tax Appellate Tribunal (CESTAT). The Explanatory Notes of the World Customs Organization and HSN are persuasive but not binding on Indian courts.

source: Customs Act 1962 — Bill of Entry
title: Bill of Entry — Types, Filing, and Requirements
text: A Bill of Entry (B/E) is the primary import declaration filed by the importer or a licensed customs broker on ICEGATE. Three types exist: (1) Bill of Entry for Home Consumption — goods cleared directly into domestic trade; (2) Bill of Entry into Bond (Into Bond B/E) — goods warehoused without payment of duty; (3) Ex-Bond Bill of Entry — goods cleared from warehouse on payment of duty. The B/E must include: IEC of importer, 8-digit ITC-HS code, declared CIF value, country of origin, supplier details, invoice number and date, and any applicable exemption notification numbers. Supporting documents required: commercial invoice, packing list, Bill of Lading/Airway Bill, certificate of origin (if FTA benefit claimed), and any PGA-specific permits (FSSAI, BIS, WPC, CDSCO). B/E must be filed within 30 days of arrival; late filing attracts interest under Section 47(2).

source: Customs Act 1962 — Import General Manifest
title: Import General Manifest (IGM) — Carrier Filing Requirement
text: The Import General Manifest (IGM) is a declaration filed by the carrier (shipping line, airline, or road carrier) with customs before or at the time of arrival of the conveyance. For sea cargo, the IGM must be filed before the vessel enters Indian territorial waters. The IGM contains details of all cargo on board including: Bill of Lading number, shipper, consignee, description of goods, number of packages, and port of loading. Once the IGM is filed, the importer can file a Bill of Entry referencing the IGM line item number. Discrepancies between the IGM and the B/E trigger customs queries and examination. The carrier is liable for goods in the manifest that are not accounted for. Amendment to IGM requires permission from the Assistant Commissioner of Customs.

source: Customs Valuation (Determination of Value of Imported Goods) Rules 2007
title: Customs Valuation — CIF Basis and Transaction Value
text: Indian customs duty is levied on the Assessable Value, which is the CIF (Cost + Insurance + Freight) value of imported goods converted to INR at the exchange rate notified by CBIC each week. This differs from the US FOB-based system. Rule 3 of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007 establishes Transaction Value as the primary method: the price actually paid or payable when goods are sold for export to India, plus any additions for commissions, royalties, assists, and proceeds of resale accruing to the seller. If the buyer and seller are related parties, the Customs Officer may refer the transaction to the Special Valuation Branch (SVB) to determine if the relationship influenced the price. Alternative valuation methods (identical goods, similar goods, deductive, computed, fallback) apply in sequence when transaction value cannot be used. Undervaluation is the most common basis for penalty proceedings under Section 111 and 112.

source: Customs Tariff Act 1975 + IGST Act 2017 — Duty Structure
title: Indian Import Duty Structure — BCD, SWS, and IGST
text: Imports into India attract multiple layers of duty: (1) Basic Customs Duty (BCD) — ad valorem rate on the Assessable Value (CIF in INR), prescribed by the Customs Tariff Act 1975 for each ITC-HS heading; (2) Social Welfare Surcharge (SWS) — 10% of BCD, levied under the Finance Act 2018; (3) Integrated Goods and Services Tax (IGST) — levied under the IGST Act 2017 at the rate applicable to the domestic supply of the same goods, calculated on (Assessable Value + BCD + SWS + Compensation Cess if any). IGST paid on imports is eligible for input tax credit (ITC) under GST if the importer is a registered GST taxpayer and the goods are used for business. Compensation Cess applies to demerit goods (tobacco, coal, luxury vehicles). All duties must be paid electronically via ICEGATE before out-of-charge is granted.

source: BIS (Compulsory Registration) Order 2012 and Amendments; BIS Act 2016
title: BIS Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS) — Mandatory for Electronics
text: The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) Compulsory Registration Scheme (CRS) mandates that specific electronics and electrical goods must carry BIS certification before they can be imported, sold, or distributed in India. The current list covers over 60 product categories including: mobile phones, smartphones, tablets, laptops, power banks, LED lights, switches, cables, televisions, set-top boxes, wireless routers, and batteries. To obtain BIS registration, the foreign manufacturer must apply to BIS, have the product tested at a BIS-recognised lab, and appoint an Authorised Indian Representative (AIR). Each product model requires a separate registration. At the port of import, customs will verify that the BIS registration number is marked on the product and in the import documents. Unregistered goods are subject to confiscation under Section 17 of the BIS Act 2016 and cannot be re-exported without BIS permission. The importer is jointly liable with the manufacturer for non-compliant goods.

source: Food Safety and Standards (Import) Regulations 2017; FSSAI Circulars
title: FSSAI Import Clearance — Mandatory for All Food and Agricultural Imports
text: Any article of food imported into India requires clearance from the Food Safety and Standards Authority of India (FSSAI) under the Food Safety and Standards (Import) Regulations 2017. The import process involves: (1) Prior Intimation — the importer must file a prior intimation on FSSAI's online platform (Food Import Management System — FIMS) before the goods arrive; (2) Document verification — FSSAI officers at the port check invoices, certificates, ingredient lists, and shelf life; (3) Physical examination and sampling — products are sampled and sent to FSSAI-notified laboratories for testing; (4) Out-of-Charge — goods are released only after lab results confirm compliance with FSSAI standards. Foreign manufacturers supplying to India must be registered on FSSAI's system. High-risk food categories (meat, dairy, processed foods, infant formula) face more stringent testing. Non-compliant goods are destroyed at the importer's cost. FSSAI clearance is required in addition to customs clearance; goods cannot leave the port until both are obtained.

source: Drugs and Cosmetics Act 1940; CDSCO Import Guidelines
title: CDSCO — Import of Drugs, Medical Devices, and Cosmetics
text: Drugs, pharmaceuticals, biological products, medical devices, and cosmetics imported into India require a No Objection Certificate (NOC) or import licence from the Central Drugs Standard Control Organisation (CDSCO). The importer must hold a valid Drug Licence (Form 10, 10A) issued by the State Drug Licensing Authority and a CDSCO import registration for each product. Medical devices are classified under Class A, B, C, or D based on risk; Class C and D devices require CDSCO approval before import. New drugs (first approved outside India within the past 4 years) require additional CDSCO regulatory review. Each consignment must be accompanied by a Certificate of Analysis from the manufacturer. Samples may be drawn at port for testing. Cosmetics require a cosmetics import licence and must comply with BIS standards and the Drugs and Cosmetics Rules 1945. Non-compliant products are liable to detention, destruction, or legal proceedings under the Drugs and Cosmetics Act.

source: Indian Wireless Telegraphy Act 1933; WPC Licensing Guidelines
title: WPC Licence — Mandatory for Wireless and Radio Equipment
text: All wireless equipment imported into India — including WiFi routers, Bluetooth devices, mobile phones with radio capability, satellite communication equipment, and radio transceivers — requires a licence from the Wireless Planning and Coordination (WPC) Wing of the Ministry of Communications before import. The importer must obtain a WPC import licence (Form WPC-4) specifying the type of equipment, quantity, and intended use. Equipment must also comply with type approval requirements under the Indian Wireless Telegraphy Act 1933. Operating unlicensed wireless equipment is a criminal offence with imprisonment up to three years. For large commercial imports, an Equipment Type Approval (ETA) from WPC and compliance with TEC (Telecom Engineering Centre) guidelines may also be required. Customs will verify the WPC licence at the time of examination before releasing wireless equipment.

source: Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act 1992; DGFT FTP 2023
title: Foreign Trade Policy 2023 — Import Licensing Framework
text: India's import policy under the Foreign Trade Policy 2023 (FTP 2023) classifies goods into three categories: (1) Free — importable without a licence (majority of goods); (2) Restricted — importable only with a licence from DGFT; (3) Prohibited — cannot be imported under any circumstances (e.g., tallow, animal fat, second-hand personal computers with CRT monitors). Canalised goods can only be imported through specified government agencies (e.g., certain petroleum products through IOC/BPCL/HPCL). The import policy for each ITC-HS code is specified in Schedule 1 of the ITC-HS Classification. Importers must check the import policy of their specific HS code on DGFT's website before placing an import order. Import licences are granted subject to conditions including end-use undertakings, export obligations (in case of Advance Authorisation), and registration on DGFT's portal.

source: DGFT — Importer Exporter Code
title: IEC — Importer Exporter Code (Mandatory for All Importers)
text: The Importer Exporter Code (IEC) is a 10-digit code issued by DGFT and is mandatory for every entity importing goods into India. No import shipment can be cleared from customs without a valid IEC. The IEC is linked to the importer's PAN (Permanent Account Number) and must be updated on the DGFT portal annually. A single IEC is sufficient for all import/export transactions, regardless of volume or commodity. IEC is also linked to the ICEGATE customs filing system and the GST registration of the importer. Banks require IEC for processing inward remittances for import payments under FEMA. Charitable and government institutions importing for non-commercial purposes may apply for an exemption. Cancellation of IEC by DGFT (for non-compliance, fraud, or on the importer's request) immediately suspends all import filing privileges.

source: DGFT Public Notice 2/2019; Customs Circular 13/2019
title: Pakistan Trade Restrictions — MFN Revocation and Import Suspension
text: India revoked Pakistan's Most Favoured Nation (MFN) trade status on 16 February 2019, following the Pulwama terrorist attack, in which 40 CRPF personnel were killed. The MFN revocation was accompanied by imposition of 200% customs duty on all goods originating in Pakistan. In August 2019, following the revocation of Article 370, India suspended all direct trade with Pakistan. Consequently, direct import of goods from Pakistan is effectively prohibited. Goods with Pakistan as the country of origin, even if shipped via third countries (UAE, Afghanistan, Singapore), are subject to enhanced scrutiny and may be treated as prohibited imports under Section 11 of the Customs Act 1962 if the claimed third-country origin cannot be verified. Importers receiving goods that may have transited Pakistan must be able to provide full chain-of-custody documentation demonstrating substantial transformation in the declared country of origin.

source: UN Security Council Resolutions; Customs Act 1962 Section 11
title: UN Sanctions — North Korea (DPRK) Import Restrictions
text: India, as a member of the United Nations, is obligated to implement mandatory sanctions imposed by the UN Security Council under Chapter VII of the UN Charter. UN Security Council Resolutions 1718 (2006), 1874 (2009), 2270 (2016), 2321 (2016), 2371 (2017), 2375 (2017), and 2397 (2017) impose comprehensive trade restrictions on the Democratic People's Republic of Korea (DPRK/North Korea). These include: prohibition on import of coal, iron, seafood, lead, textiles, food, and agricultural products from DPRK; prohibition on export of refined petroleum and crude oil to DPRK; financial transaction restrictions. The Ministry of External Affairs and CBIC issue implementation notifications under the Customs Act 1962 Section 11. Any shipment with DPRK-origin goods or involving DPRK entities must be immediately held and referred to the Commissioner of Customs and the Ministry of External Affairs.

source: Customs Tariff Act 1975 Section 9A; DGTR Anti-Dumping Notifications
title: Anti-Dumping Duties — DGTR Orders and Provisional Assessment
text: India imposes anti-dumping duties on specific goods from specific countries when the Directorate General of Trade Remedies (DGTR) determines that such goods are being exported to India below normal value, causing material injury to the domestic industry. Anti-dumping duties are notified by the Ministry of Finance (Department of Revenue) and are in addition to Basic Customs Duty and IGST. Active anti-dumping investigations and final orders are published on the DGTR website. Major product categories with ADD orders against China include: certain chemicals, steel and aluminium products, synthetic fibres, ceramics, electronic components, and rubber products. Importers must check the DGTR order database for their specific product and country before filing the Bill of Entry. The ADD rate varies by manufacturer/exporter; goods from producers not specifically named in the order are typically assessed at the residual (highest) rate. ADD orders typically remain valid for 5 years and may be extended after a sunset review.

source: Customs Tariff Act 1975 Section 8B; CBIC Safeguard Notifications
title: Safeguard Duties — Temporary Protection for Domestic Industry
text: Safeguard duties are temporary additional customs duties imposed by the Indian government to protect domestic industries from a sudden surge in imports that causes or threatens serious injury. They are administered under Section 8B of the Customs Tariff Act 1975. Unlike anti-dumping duties, safeguard duties apply to imports from all countries (with limited GSP/FTA exceptions). The Directorate General of Trade Remedies (DGTR) investigates safeguard complaints; once confirmed, a Safeguard Duty Notification is issued by the Ministry of Finance. Prominent examples include safeguard duties on solar cells and modules, certain steel products, and optic fibre cables. Importers of affected goods must check the CBIC Customs Tariff Notifications database before importing. Safeguard duties are subject to definitive review and may be reduced progressively during their validity period.

source: DGFT SCOMET List, Appendix 3, Schedule 2 ITC-HS; FTDR Act 1992
title: SCOMET — Dual-Use, Defence, and Strategic Items Controls
text: India controls the import and export of Special Chemicals, Organisms, Materials, Equipment and Technologies (SCOMET) under the Foreign Trade (Development and Regulation) Act 1992 and the SCOMET Policy. The SCOMET list mirrors international export control regimes (Wassenaar Arrangement, Australia Group, Missile Technology Control Regime, Nuclear Suppliers Group). Imports of SCOMET items require prior authorisation from DGFT. The importer must submit an End-Use Certificate (EUC) and End-User Certificate (EUC), detailing the intended use and ultimate user. Post-import reporting to DGFT is mandatory. SCOMET categories cover: nuclear materials and equipment, biological agents, chemical weapons precursors, dual-use electronics and computers, aerospace and propulsion systems, defence articles (weapons, ammunition, military vehicles), and directed energy weapons. Unauthorised import of SCOMET items is punishable under the FTDR Act 1992 with imprisonment up to 5 years, a fine up to five times the value of goods, and confiscation.

source: India-UAE Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement (CEPA) 2022
title: India-UAE CEPA — Preferential Duty and Rules of Origin
text: The India-UAE Comprehensive Economic Partnership Agreement (CEPA), effective 1 May 2022, provides preferential customs duty rates for qualifying goods traded between India and the UAE. For imports into India from the UAE, the importer must present a Certificate of Origin (CoO) issued by an authorised UAE body (e.g., Dubai Chamber, Abu Dhabi Chamber) to claim the preferential rate. Rules of origin under CEPA require that goods either originate in the UAE or undergo sufficient processing there (tariff shift or regional value content criterion). Gold and jewellery imports from the UAE under CEPA are subject to a 1% BCD (reduced from 15%), but are subject to a tariff rate quota (TRQ) beyond which normal rates apply. Textiles and apparel from the UAE benefit from significant duty concessions subject to a yarn-forward rule of origin. Importers must retain CoO for 5 years for potential DGFT verification.

source: ASEAN-India Free Trade Agreement (AIFTA) 2010; Rules of Origin
title: ASEAN-India FTA — Duty Concessions and Rules of Origin
text: The ASEAN-India Free Trade Agreement (AIFTA), effective from 2010, provides preferential customs duty rates for goods traded between India and the 10 ASEAN member states (Indonesia, Malaysia, Thailand, Philippines, Vietnam, Singapore, Myanmar, Cambodia, Laos, Brunei). To claim AIFTA preferential duty, importers must present an ASEAN-India Form AI Certificate of Origin issued by an authorised ASEAN government body. Rules of origin require either: (a) wholly produced in the exporting ASEAN country, or (b) at least 35% regional value content (RVC) based on FOB price, or (c) change in tariff sub-heading at the 4-digit level. Certain sensitive goods (rice, plantation products, some textiles) are excluded from preferential treatment. India has ongoing concerns about trade deflection through ASEAN; DGFT may require additional proof of substantial transformation for electronics and chemicals transshipped through ASEAN from China. Origin verification requests can be sent to the exporting country's authorities.

source: DGFT — Advance Authorisation Scheme; Handbook of Procedures FTP 2023
title: Advance Authorisation — Duty-Free Import for Export Production
text: The Advance Authorisation (AA) scheme under the Foreign Trade Policy 2023 allows exporters to import inputs, including fuel and oil, duty-free (exempt from BCD, SWS, and IGST) when used in the manufacture of goods for export. The authorisation is issued by DGFT based on the Standard Input-Output Norms (SION) for the export product. Key conditions: (1) the authorisation holder must fulfill the export obligation within 18 months (extendable) from the date of issue; (2) imported goods must be used only for the specified export product; (3) the authorisation holder must maintain proper records of consumption; (4) on completion of export obligation, the holder must obtain a EODC (Export Obligation Discharge Certificate) from DGFT. Failure to meet export obligations results in recovery of foregone duties with interest at 15% per annum plus penalties. The scheme is subject to actual user condition — the goods cannot be sold or transferred.

source: Special Economic Zones Act 2005; SEZ Rules 2006
title: SEZ Import Procedures — Customs Procedures for Special Economic Zones
text: Goods imported into a Special Economic Zone (SEZ) in India are treated as imports into a customs-bonded territory and are exempt from BCD, SWS, IGST, and all import duties at the time of import. The SEZ unit must file a Bill of Entry (into SEZ) on ICEGATE. Goods are permitted into the SEZ without payment of duty on execution of a Bond-cum-Legal Undertaking (BLUT) by the SEZ developer/unit. Goods removed from the SEZ into the Domestic Tariff Area (DTA) are treated as imports and attract full applicable duties. SEZ units operating as Export Oriented Units have additional obligations including minimum net foreign exchange earnings. Goods in transit through Indian customs territory to an SEZ must move under customs seal. A Development Commissioner authorises the SEZ operations; CBIC and the Development Commissioner jointly supervise customs compliance within the SEZ.

source: Customs Act 1962 Sections 74 and 75; Customs and Central Excise Duties Drawback Rules 2017
title: Customs Duty Drawback — Refund on Re-Export or Export of Imported Goods
text: Customs duty drawback allows recovery of customs duty paid on imported goods that are subsequently re-exported or used in manufacture of exported goods. Two types: (1) Section 74 drawback — 98% of duty paid on imported goods that are re-exported in the same or substantially the same condition within 2 years of import; the goods must not have been used in India, or if used, must have been used for a limited period; (2) Section 75 drawback — all-industry rates or brand rates for duty paid on inputs used in manufacturing exported products, administered by CBIC Drawback Directorate. Drawback claims must be filed within 3 months of export (extendable to 12 months with payment of fee). IGST refunds on exports are processed separately through the GST portal. Drawback on goods exported without BIS/FSSAI/WPC compliance for the domestic market is not affected, but the goods must comply with the importing country's standards.

source: Customs Act 1962 Sections 111 and 112 — Penalty Framework
title: Customs Penalties — Confiscation (Section 111) and Personal Penalty (Section 112)
text: Section 111 of the Customs Act 1962 lists goods liable to confiscation, including: goods imported without a valid IEC, goods misdeclared as to quantity, value, or description, goods in violation of import prohibitions or restrictions, goods under-valued to evade duty, and smuggled goods. Section 112 prescribes personal penalty for any person who acquires, possesses, or deals in goods liable to confiscation, or who is connected with their import. Penalty amounts: for goods where the value of goods is determined — up to the value of the goods or five times the duty payable, whichever is greater; for cases not involving duty evasion — up to INR 1 lakh or five times the value of the goods. Mens rea (intention) is not required for confiscation under Section 111 but is relevant to penalty under Section 112. The adjudicating authority has discretion to allow redemption of confiscated goods on payment of a Redemption Fine in lieu of confiscation. Orders are appealable to Commissioner (Appeals), CESTAT, High Court, and Supreme Court.

source: Customs Act 1962 Section 14A; Customs Circular — Special Valuation Branch
title: Special Valuation Branch (SVB) — Related Party Transactions
text: When the buyer and seller of imported goods are related parties (as defined in Rule 2(2) of the Customs Valuation Rules 2007 — e.g., parent-subsidiary, common directors, partnership, affiliates), the Customs Officer may refer the transaction to the Special Valuation Branch (SVB) to examine whether the relationship has influenced the declared price. While the reference is pending, the importer may clear goods provisionally against a bond (typically 1-4% of the assessable value as a provisional deposit). SVB investigation examines transfer pricing documentation, cost structures, profit margins, and comparable uncontrolled prices. If the SVB finds that the transaction value is acceptable, the provisional deposit is refunded. If the price is found to be influenced, the customs officer will reject transaction value and apply alternative methods. SVB orders must be renewed every three years. Multinational importers and subsidiaries importing from parent companies should proactively prepare transfer pricing documentation.

source: Customs Act 1962 Schedule; DGFT FTP 2023 — Prohibited and Restricted Goods
title: Prohibited and Restricted Imports — Schedule to Customs Act
text: The Customs Act 1962 and the Foreign Trade Policy 2023 collectively define goods that are prohibited or restricted from import into India. Absolutely prohibited goods include: narcotic drugs (without valid licence), obscene material, counterfeit currency, wildlife articles covered by CITES, hazardous waste (subject to exceptions under Basel Convention), and goods bearing the name or flag of an enemy country. Restricted goods (requiring DGFT import licence) include: second-hand goods (certain categories), arms and ammunition (requiring Home Ministry approval), nuclear materials, ozone-depleting substances, hazardous chemicals, live animals and plants subject to CITES, and certain electronic wastes. Canalised imports (through designated agencies only) include certain petroleum products, fertilisers, and cereals. The import policy for each ITC-HS code is maintained in the Schedule 1 database on DGFT's website and is updated periodically through DGFT notifications and amendments to the Foreign Trade Policy.

source: CBIC Risk Management System; Customs Act 1962 Section 17
title: Customs Examination and Risk Management System (RMS)
text: The Risk Management System (RMS) is CBIC's automated cargo targeting tool that assigns a risk score to every Bill of Entry filed on ICEGATE and determines the examination channel. Four channels: (1) Green (Facilitated) — no examination, documents checked only; (2) Yellow — documentary examination by the Appraising Officer; (3) Orange — physical examination of 5-10% of packages; (4) Red — 100% examination with detailed scrutiny. Factors driving higher risk scores include: first-time importer, mismatch between declared value and market price, high-risk country of origin, commodity prone to misdeclaration, and absence of prior import history. C-TPAT-equivalent trusted trader programs in India (Authorised Economic Operator — AEO) provide facilitation benefits similar to green-channel treatment. AEO-T1, T2, and T3 tiers offer progressively higher facilitation. Importers can apply for AEO status through CBIC's AEO Programme; compliance history, financial solvency, and supply chain security standards are evaluated.
